title: Top Five Film Scores. Plus: Hans Zimmer on 'Dune'!
format: A bonus episode built around a category the Oscars cut from the broadcast. Sean and Pitchfork critic Jason Green work through the Best Original Score nominees and argue over which composers are changing film music, then trade top-five lists before Hans Zimmer explains the instruments he had to invent for Dune.

segments.heading: This Year's Best Original Score Race

films.title: Dune
films.note: The nominee everyone expects to win; Zimmer built new instruments to score it, discussed later in the episode.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Jonny Greenwood's score plays in counterpoint to the movie instead of alongside it.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: Nicholas Britell's score gets more credit than the movie for landing its satire.

films.title: Parallel Mothers
films.note: Alberto Iglesias's nomination for the Almodovar film was the category's biggest surprise.

films.title: Encanto
films.note: Germaine Franco's score, overshadowed by Lin-Manuel Miranda's songs.

segments.heading: The Composers Changing Film Music
segments.note: Mica Levi, Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross, Michael Giacchino

films.title: Under the Skin
films.note: Mica Levi's breakout score, built on strings written around a chord rather than in one.

films.title: Zola
films.note: Levi again, this time closer to a Twitter thread's rhythm than an orchestra.

films.title: The Social Network
films.note: Reznor and Ross's archetypal score, still Green's go-to writing music.

films.title: Soul
films.note: Reznor and Ross again, for the film's in-between space.

films.title: Mank
films.note: A love letter to the 1930s European emigre composers, built the same year as Soul.

films.title: Annihilation
films.note: Ben Salisbury and Geoff Barrow's score, indebted to Barrow's Portishead work.

films.title: The Batman
films.note: Michael Giacchino's score turns Batman's wet boots into a rhythm section.

films.title: The Incredibles
films.note: Giacchino's 1960s spy-show pastiche theme.

films.title: Inside Out
films.note: Giacchino's string theme for the film both hosts call devastating.

segments.heading: Jason Green's Top Five Film Scores
segments.subject: Film Scores

films.title: The Empire Strikes Back
films.note: No. 1 - the Imperial March and the Han-and-Leia theme both debut here, not in the original Star Wars.

films.title: Blade Runner
films.note: No. 2 - Vangelis on the Yamaha CS-80, a synth so expensive it was basically a car payment.

films.title: Rocky
films.note: No. 3 - Bill Conti's theme convinces you, for one round, that the loser can win.

films.title: Klute
films.note: No. 4 - Michael Small's paranoid xylophone cue was later sampled directly into Homecoming.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: No. 5 - Bernard Herrmann's last score, written in two days; he died before the Oscars.

segments.heading: Sean Fennessey's Top Five Film Scores
segments.subject: Film Scores
segments.note: crossover pick: Klute, taken by Green above, was also on Sean's list

films.title: The Third Man
films.note: No. 1 - a street zither player scoring a film about societal rot, against every instinct of the genre.

films.title: There Will Be Blood
films.note: No. 2 - Jonny Greenwood's Penderecki-indebted score, all impending doom.

films.title: Halloween
films.note: No. 3 - John Carpenter wrote it on one synthesizer over a weekend because he didn't want to pay a composer.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in the West
films.note: No. 5 - Morricone's harmonica motif that only pays off at the final showdown.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Zimmer turned down the David Lynch adaptation and the TV series to protect his own mental image of the book, then built the score around invented instruments and Loa Kotler's voice rather than character themes.

referenced.group: Herrmann's other Hitchcock work, cited in passing
films: The Day the Earth Stood Still
Once Upon a Time in America
Close Encounters of the Third Kind
Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone

referenced.group: Elmer Bernstein and Jerry Goldsmith, cited in passing
Lin-Manuel Miranda and Pixar, cited in passing
